sections.text: Welcome back. Algebra One, Module Three — properties of real numbers. The named shortcuts you've been using your whole life without knowing they had names. Today we name them, because they let you simplify any expression in any direction.

Quick check. Eight plus five. Five plus eight. Same answer, right? Of course. You did not stop to think about it. That instinct — the one that tells you order doesn't matter for adding — has a name. It's the commutative property. Today we name five of these instincts. Once you see them written down, you'll start spotting where algebra is using them to do work.

Five properties to cover. Commutative. Associative. Distributive. Identity. Inverse. Every one of these you've used since elementary school. Today we make them official, and I'll show you what each one looks like in algebra, not just arithmetic.

Commutative property. Order doesn't matter. For addition — a plus b equals b plus a. For multiplication — a times b equals b times a. So three plus seven and seven plus three both give ten. Two times five and five times two both give ten. Notice — this works for adding and multiplying only. Subtraction and division do NOT commute. Five minus three is two. Three minus five is negative two. Different answers.

Associative property. Grouping doesn't matter. For addition — the quantity a plus b, plus c, equals a plus the quantity b plus c. Same idea for multiplication. So two plus three plus four — you can add two and three first, or three and four first. Same answer, nine. Like commutative, this only works for addition and multiplication.

Distributive property. The mighty one. a times the quantity b plus c, equals a-b plus a-c. The multiplication distributes across the addition. Three times the quantity x plus four equals three x plus twelve. This is what lets you 'multiply out' parentheses, and it's how almost every Algebra simplification gets started.

Identity property. Two flavors. Additive identity is zero — anything plus zero is itself. x plus zero equals x. Multiplicative identity is one — anything times one is itself. x times one equals x. Sounds trivial, right? It's actually how we add and remove zero or one strategically inside an expression to simplify, without changing its value.

Inverse property. The undo-er. Additive inverse is the negative — x plus negative x equals zero. Multiplicative inverse is the reciprocal — x times one over x equals one. This is the property that justifies 'subtract from both sides' and 'divide both sides' from Module Four. Behind every step where we cancel something out, the inverse property is doing the work.

Your turn. Which property is being used here? The expression two plus the quantity x plus five gets rewritten as the quantity two plus x, plus five. Pause and decide which property that is. Hint — nothing's getting multiplied out, but the parentheses moved.

Time. Associative property. The grouping changed but the order of the numbers stayed the same. If the order had also flipped, that would be commutative. If parentheses got distributed across, that would be distributive. Reading the difference between these is exactly what your assignment will test.

Why these matter in real algebra. Take the expression three x plus two plus five x minus seven. To simplify, you commute the like terms next to each other, then associate them into groups, then add. You used three properties in one move and didn't notice. Properties are the rules behind every legal step you take in algebra. Knowing their names makes proofs and word problems readable.

Recap. Commutative — order doesn't matter for plus and times. Associative — grouping doesn't matter for plus and times. Distributive — multiplication spreads across addition. Identity — zero for plus, one for times. Inverse — every number has an additive inverse, every nonzero number has a multiplicative inverse. Five properties. They're the grammar of algebra.

To master this — read OpenStax Chapters one point three through one point five. Run the Khan Academy properties practice set. Your assignment in the dashboard has ten algebraic steps; you decide which property justifies each one. That's the test that reveals whether you really see them. Light module. See you in Module Four — solving one-step and two-step equations, where these properties start doing actual work.
